A COMPARATIVE STUDY OF TWO Al-Mg-Si ALLOYS FOR AUTOMOTIVE APPLICATIONS

T. Moons, P. Ratchev, P. De Smet $^{\S}$ , B. Verlinden, P. Van Houtte  
Department of Metallurgy and Materials Engineering, Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, de Croylaan 2, B-3001 Leuven, Belgium

Hoogovens Aluminium N.V., A.Stocletlaan 87, B-2570 Duffel, Belgium

(Received May 3, 1996)

Introduction

In search for lighter cars, the automotive industry shows a considerable interest in the application of aluminium sheets for car body panels. The basic requirement for those sheets is to have a high formability, so that the panels can be stamped, while retaining or preferably increasing their strength when the part is painted and baked. The heat treatable Al-Mg-Si alloys of the 6xxx series are often used. They have a very good formability, corrosion resistance, spot weldability and additionally show a precipitate strengthening after the paint-bake cycle (1).

The relationship between the $\mathrm{Mg_2Si}$ precipitates and the mechanical properties of these alloys is well established. Coherent needle-shaped $\beta$ - $\mathrm{Mg_2Si}$ precipitates which play a key role in the strengthening mechanism evolve from the solid solution during the baking treatment. According to Lorimer (2) the precipitation sequence in Al-Mg-Si alloys is as follows:

supersaturated $\mathbf{a} \rightarrow$ clusters $\rightarrow$ needle-shaped GPZ $(\mathbf{b}^{\prime \prime}) \rightarrow$ rods $(\mathbf{b}^{\prime}) \rightarrow$ equilibrium $\mathrm{Mg}_2\mathrm{Si}$ plates.

The formability and strength of aluminium alloys is inferior compared with steel. This necessitates to acquire a better understanding of the link between processing, microstructure and mechanical properties of these alloys. The present work is a comparative study of two Al-Mg-Si alloys which differ in the level of Si. The influence of ageing, deformation and a second solution treatment on the mechanical properties has been studied. The associated microstructures, evaluated by means of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray microanalysis (EDS), have been used to explain the observed properties.

Experimental

The composition of the two alloys under investigation is given in Table 1. One of them is a commercial 6016 alloy and the other is an experimental 6xxx alloy supplied by Hoogovens Aluminium. Ten-

sile test samples along the rolling direction were cut, solution treated at 823K for 60 seconds and water quenched (WQ). The mechanical properties have been measured by means of tensile tests:

TEM samples were prepared by double jet electro-polishing in $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$ solution (volume ratio 1:3) at a temperature of $243\mathrm{K}$ and a voltage of $9\mathrm{V}$ . The TEM and EDS analysis have been performed with a Philips CM 200FEG at $200\mathrm{kV}$ . The quantification of the $\beta^{\prime \prime} - \mathrm{Mg}_{2}\mathrm{Si}$ particles number density, which is related to the peak of hardness, has been performed according to the procedure outlined in (3) and (4). It consists of viewing the sample along the $<100>$ matrix zone axis and counting of the $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ -needles aligned along this axis (this is one third of the total). In order to find the corresponding volume the thickness of the analysed area was measured by means of converged beam electron diffraction (CBED). A correction, taking into account the fact that some of the needles crossing the specimen surface and belonging to a missing volume are counted, is applied. For this the average length of the needles is measured. The volume fraction of the $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ -precipitates has been calculated using a cylinder approximation and for this the average particle cross section has been measured.

Results and Discussion

The influence of the artificial ageing temperature is given in fig. 1 and fig. 2, where the yield strength and the uniform elongation are given as function of the ageing temperature. Ageing at $453\mathrm{K}$ , after 10 days of natural ageing, does not affect the mechanical properties of either alloys. Alloy 6061 shows

about $20\%$ higher yield strength than the experimental 6xxx alloy, but the same ductility. After ageing at 478K both alloys show a drastic increase of strength and a decrease of ductility. It is well established that the coherent $\beta^{\prime \prime} - \mathrm{Mg}_2\mathrm{Si}$ precipitates, which form during artificial ageing, are responsible for the

The Volume Fraction was Calculated Using a Cylinder Approximation (the Measured Average Cross Section for the 3 Measured Samples is $10~\mu \mathrm{m}^2$

precipitation strengthening in Al-Mg-Si alloys, see ref.(3). In fig. 3 (a and b) the microstructure of the two alloys after ageing at $478\mathrm{K}$ is given. The TEM micrographs are taken in $<100>$ matrix orientation, which is the most suitable for $\beta$ "MgSi phase visualisation. A homogeneous precipitation of $\beta$ " phase is clearly visible. Fig. 3 reveals a higher volume fraction of precipitates in alloy 6016 than in alloy 6xxx after $478\mathrm{K} / 30\mathrm{min}$ ageing. The results of the quantification of the number density and the volume fractions of these alloys are summarised in Table 2. It can be clearly concluded that the higher yield strength of alloy 6016 after ageing at $478\mathrm{K}$ is linked with the much higher volume fraction of the $\beta$ " phase precipitates. On the other hand a $\beta$ " precipitation after natural or artificial ageing at $453\mathrm{K}$ for 30 min was not found. According to Huppert (5) ternary Al-Mg-Si clusters form during the natural ageing. These clusters are the cause of age hardening in T4 condition. They are disordered and can not be observed neither by electron microscopy nor by electron diffraction. Since the diffusion velocity of Si atoms is much smaller than the one of the Mg atoms, the amount of Si determines the number of nuclei for the clusters formation. So, the higher yield strength of alloy 6016 after natural or artificial ageing at $453\mathrm{K}$ for $30\mathrm{min}$ is an effect of cluster strengthening due to the higher amount of Si in comparison to the experimental 6xxx alloy.

The influence of the predeformation (2 and $5\%$ ) on the yield strength for the two ageing temperatures is shown in fig. 4. It can be seen that after the predeformation and ageing at $453\mathrm{K}$ for 30 minutes the yield strength of both alloys has increased in an almost linear way with the increase of predeformation. Since it was confirmed by TEM that homogeneous $\beta$ precipitation is not taking place at this baking condition, the increase in yield strength is due to work hardening. This becomes more clear when the yield strengths after predeformations of respectively 0, 2 and $5\%$ followed by baking at

[caption] Figure 7. The formability in T4 condition is not improved with the second solution treatment.

453K, are compared with the flow stress at 0.2, 2 and $5\%$ of deformation during a tensile test in T4 condition, see Table 3. For the alloy 6016 the influence of baking at 453K is zero MPa without predeformation and $2\mathrm{MPa}$ and $13\mathrm{MPa}$ after 2 and $5\%$ of predeformation. Ageing at 478K shows completely different results. The yield strength of both alloys is increasing until $2\%$ of predeformation and then there is a plateau until $5\%$ of deformation. Here, there is a combination of two effects: work and precipitation hardening. From Table 3 can be seen that for alloy 6016 the strengthening due to the baking at 478K is respectively 118, 108 and $73\mathrm{MPa}$ after 0, 2 and $5\%$ of predeformation. The strengthening after $0\%$ of predeformation is clearly an effect of $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ -phase precipitation and after 2 and $5\%$ of predeformation is a combined effect of work and precipitation strengthening. Surprisingly, the last two values are smaller than the first. The explanation of this fact can be as follows. It has been shown (6) that not only homogeneous $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ precipitation but also heterogeneous $\mathrm{Mg_2Si}$ precipitation takes place on dislocations in samples deformed and baked at 473K. Since the heterogeneous precipitates do not contribute to the material strength, a certain part of the strengthening possibilities is lost. With increasing amount of deformation more dislocations are introduced into the material and a larger amount of $\mathrm{Mg_2Si}$ might precipitate heterogenously and subsequently the material shows a smaller precipitation hardening. So, in the case of ageing at 478K the increase in the work hardening with the deformation is almost compensated by a decrease due to smaller precipitation hardening, which results in a yield strength that is relatively insensitive to the amount of predeformation (241, 260 and $263\mathrm{MPa}$ after 0, 2 and $5\%$ of deformation for alloy 6016). A second possible explanation is linked to the fact that predeformation accelerates the precipitation hardening and is shifting the peak of hardness towards smaller baking times (6). If we suppose that the maximum hardness is reached at a deformation of $2\%$ and baking at 478K for 30 minutes, it is logical that after $5\%$ of predeformation under the same baking conditions the material is already overaged and shows much less precipitation hardening. The last reasoning, although logical, is the less probable because, according to (6), the peak of hardness is situated around 1h or more of ageing for similar materials, deformation and baking conditions. A baking time of 30 minutes is used in the present work which means that the material is generally in underaged condition. Thus, it is more consistent to link the loss of precipitation hardening in the present conditions with a heterogeneous $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ phase precipitation. In general the alloy 6016 show a larger strength than alloy 6xxx. The relative difference between both alloys is larger after baking at 478K because of the better precipitation hardening possibilities of alloy 6016, as shown before.

To a second group of samples a second solution treatment at 763K was given. The reason for this treatment is the claim (7) that a thermal treatment in the two phase (solid solution + Si) region is leading to precipitation of fine Si precipitates, which give a better formability in T4 conditions. The treatment was performed for 30s and 2 hours at 763K which is in the two phase region for the particular alloys compositions. The microstructure of alloy 6016 after 2h at 763K is given in fig. 5(a). The irregular micron size precipitates on the grain boundaries are identified by X-ray microanalysis as pure

944

A COMPARATIVE STUDY OF TWO Al-Mg-Si ALLOYS

Vol. 35, No. 8

Si, fig. 5(b). Similar precipitates are found also in the experimental 6xxx alloy but with much smaller density.

Fine precipitates of submicron size after this treatment were not observed. The yield strength of both alloys after ageing at 478K for $30\mathrm{min}$ as function of the time at 763K is given in fig. 6. The tendency of loosing strength is clear for both alloys and is related with the lack of $\mathrm{Mg_2Si}$ precipitates, because of the precipitation of pure Si during the heat treatment at 763K. Fig. 3(a and c) clearly reveals a smaller volume fraction of $\beta$ precipitates in alloy 6016 in T6 condition compared with the same but without the second thermal treatment. The formability in T4 conditions (the uniform elongation) as function of the time at 763K is given in fig. 7. It can be seen that a better formability is not achieved by this treatment. The only result of this treatment is an important reduction of the hardening possibilities after a subsequent ageing treatment at 478K.
